full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There are at least seven characters in this parable of the good Samaritan, because you've got to remember to count the innkeeper.<|10.22|> <|11.16|> But if there's seven, I'm only counting two for the robbers.<|17.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but there could be many more, just as plural for robbers.<|4.08|> <|4.18|> But let's be safe in count two, although the road from Jerusalem to Jericho was notorious for being very dangerous<|11.78|> <|11.78|> and just filled with robbers everywhere.<|14.50|> <|15.54|> The number seven doesn't include Jesus or the lawyer.<|18.98|> <|18.98|> They're not in the parable, so we'll leave them out.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The two characters that I want to start with are the priest and the Levite, not the main characters, but the two who usually get a bad rap because they don't cross over to the other side to help the nameless man who has been left for dead after being stripped and beaten and presumably robbed.<|21.24|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's often said by Christian preachers, and it's been said for centuries, that the<|5.92|> <|5.92|> reason why the priest and the Levite didn't cross over to help the man is<|12.16|> <|12.16|> because they were afraid of being in the presence, or God forbid, touching a corpse,<|17.16|> <|17.16|> and Jewish law forbids it, and they would have been deemed impure.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> This is a standard trope on this parable.<|25.20|> <|25.20|> The problem with it is it's wrong.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and self-serving. Amy Jill Levine is a Jewish theologian and scholar of the Old and New<|8.34|> <|8.34|> Testaments who teaches at Vanderbilt. She has a great book on the parables of Jesus. The title<|14.42|> <|14.42|> alone is wonderful. It's called Short Stories by Jesus.<|18.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And she says that Jewish law actually would have required the priest and the Levite to go help the man.<|12.66|> <|13.66|> She says that Jewish law so values the saving of a human life,<|18.94|> <|19.54|> that the saving of a life overrides in Jewish law every other concern, including keeping the Sabbath.<|26.92|> <|26.92|> Sabbath.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So the problem was not their faith or Jewish law.<|3.78|> <|4.00|> Furthermore, she writes, if the man would have died, Jewish law would have required them to give him a proper and reverent burial.<|13.64|> <|15.08|> Why then didn't they go and help?<|17.88|> <|17.88|> Why?<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The law is not the problem.<|2.06|> <|2.82|> The more obvious and likely answer is they were afraid of the robbers, of course.<|8.70|> <|9.48|> Don't forget the robbers.<|11.04|> <|11.60|> They were likely terrified, just afraid to walk over and help.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> And that's something that we often can identify with, too.<|19.80|> <|20.50|> Often it's just fear that keeps us back.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The two main characters are, of course, that unnamed man and the Samaritan.<|5.88|> <|6.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On our grounds of the cathedral are 49 units of permanent supportive housing where service occurs, but also things like meals and bingo and trips to Episcopal Public Policy Network, which looks upstream at how...<|21.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Policies are created, laws are created in our country<|3.80|> <|3.80|> and advocates for bipartisan and more just laws<|8.02|> <|8.02|> so that downstream people have help and relief on a larger scale.<|13.18|> <|13.92|> All of these and other ways of serving matter and matter to God.<|19.66|> <|19.80|> Let us know if we can help you roll up your sleeves<|23.00|> <|23.00|> and serve in some new ways.<|25.46|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I know, and Deacon Jack knows and appreciates, that the vast majority of the members of the<|6.56|> <|6.56|> cathedral serve outside the cathedral. And that's a great thing. It doesn't count more.<|13.36|> <|13.88|> God doesn't love it more if it's done in church. It matters everywhere. So many of you, most of you<|19.84|> <|19.84|> serve in and through your own jobs, your own neighborhoods, your own places where you volunteer.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.70|> May this parable, the Good Samaritan, inspire you to keep going and to see and feel why it matters and even matters to God.<|11.12|> <|11.78|> And for those among us who have your own needs and need to relieve some burdens and some pain,<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> let us know what's going on<|3.22|> <|3.22|> and we'll try to do all we can to help<|5.10|> <|5.10|> or to get you to the place where you can find better help<|8.08|> <|8.08|> I know, I really do know<|10.98|> <|10.98|> that it's hard to ask for help sometimes<|14.36|> <|14.36|> but I encourage you to do so<|16.14|> <|16.14|> for loving yourself is a part of our Lord's command too<|21.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|4.18|><|endoftext|>
